retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Winter classification
retrieved_docs.content: The Winter classification describes mandibular 3rd molars based on the angulation of the crown with respect to the long axis of the 2nd molar. The Winter classification is as follows: Vertical Mesioangular Distoangular Horizontal Buccolingual Inverted Other Orientation Horizontal and distoangular impactions are more difficult to extract, as the path of delivery of the tooth is obstructed by overlying bone and the ascending ramus.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Teeth -- Contraindications -- Antiresorptive Agents
retrieved_docs.content: A thorough dental assessment should be conducted before a patient begins a course of antiresorptive drugs. This allows any tooth with a poor prognosis to be removed before introducing MRONJ risk. However, it cannot always be avoided that a tooth requires extraction during or after antiresorptive treatment. Therefore, in these scenarios, a thorough risk vs. benefit discussion must be had with the patient prior to planning for an extraction.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Indications for extraction of a lower third molar include recurrent episodes of pericoronitis, unrestorable caries, or caries extending into the pulp. Furthermore, a horizontal or mesioangular lower third molar causing disto-cervical caries in the lower second molar is indicated for extraction to facilitate restorative treatment in the lower second molar. In addition, extraction is also indicated in third molars with odontogenic cysts or tumors, those impeding surgery (such as in patients undergoing orthognathic surgery), or in the line of a mandibular fracture. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Trismus -- Etiology -- Trismus As a Complication of Dental Extractions
retrieved_docs.content: Pain, trismus, and swelling are common complications of the surgical extraction of mandibular third molars. [5] Such complications result from the inflammatory response to surgical trauma and are classically short-term. [5] [6] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Lower third molars are the most frequently extracted teeth, accounting for 18% of dental extractions. [1] [2] This is due to their posterior position intra-orally, coupled with the high likelihood of impaction, often resulting in pericoronitis. Pericoronitis is the inflammation of the soft tissues surrounding the crown of a partially erupted tooth. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Clinical Significance -- Extraction of Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: CBCT imaging must only be indicated in specific cases where the operator has a clinical question that panoramic or intraoral radiographs cannot answer. [17] Up-to-date data shows that using CBCT before removing mandibular third molars does not reduce nerve injury or lead to better patient outcomes than panoramic radiographs. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Anatomy : There are 20 deciduous teeth (“baby teeth”) and 32 permanent teeth (generally depending on third molar development or wisdom teeth) that start to appear in the mouth around 6 years of age. The last four permanent teeth to erupt are third molars or "wisdom teeth," each of which may or may not grow in. Among deciduous teeth, ten usually are found in the maxilla (upper jaw), and ten are in the mandible (lower jaw). For permanent teeth, 16 are in the maxilla, and 16 are in the mandible.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The Benex device (Benex, Lucerne, Switzerland) is the most frequently cited vertical extraction system on the market today. However, some notable disadvantages of the Benex system are that the technique is time-consuming, and its mechanism is achievable only by counterbalancing against the dental arch, placing adjacent teeth and alveolar bone at risk for accidental fracture. [25] Additionally, the system is costly when compared to more conventional armamentaria. For these reasons, vertical extraction systems may not be the most practical means for the atraumatic extraction of teeth and roots. On the contrary, some have suggested a more cost-effective alternative to the Benex extraction system using a similar variation of the vertical extraction principle used by Benex. The technique requires using a miniplate screw fixated within the radicular canal and with a wire wound under the head of the screw. The wire-screw-root combination extrudes coronally using a wire twister and the nearby palatal bone as the fulcrum. [27] [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Dislocation -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Syringe Method: This is performed by placing an empty 5 or 10 mL syringe, oriented transversely, on one side in the posterior portion of the patient's mouth, between the upper and lower molars or posterior gums. The patient is directed to bite down softly and roll the syringe backward and forward. This maneuver gets repeated until the mandible reduces on that side. The opposite side can reduce spontaneously; however, if it does not, the syringe can be placed on the opposite side of the mouth, and the technique repeated. Syringe size is chosen based on which size can fit most easily. Procedural sedation or anesthesia is not a requirement for this technique. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Tranexamic Acid -- Indications -- Intravenous
retrieved_docs.content: Orthognathic surgery: A double-blind, randomized, controlled trial of elective bi-maxillary osteotomy, where participants received either TXA or normal saline. Their results revealed statistically significant blood loss in the TXA group; however, there was no difference in the number of blood transfusions. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Teeth -- Contraindications -- Antiresorptive Agents
retrieved_docs.content: Bisphosphonates can be administered in various ways, including orally and intravenously. A thorough history of bisphosphonate type, administration, and duration of use must be taken, as this will dictate the patient's risk of developing MRONJ. Higher risk factors have been identified, including over five years of use, concurrent steroid use, and advanced patient age. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Contraindications of extraction of lower third molars teeth are high risk of IAN damage, complex root morphology, hypercementosis, and ankylosis. In addition, third molars would not be extracted in patients with a history of intravenous bisphosphonate use or radiotherapy. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Teeth -- Indications -- Fractured Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: Fractured teeth that cannot be appropriately restored or retained will require extraction. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Due to the surgical complexity of third molar surgery, post-operative pain, swelling, and trismus often occur as a result. These effects are transient and typically well-managed with simple analgesia. Other complications include alveolar osteitis, also known as dry socket, which is managed with careful irrigation of the extraction socket with chlorhexidine gluconate or saline and dressing the socket with an obtundent, resorbable material. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Teeth -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: There are few absolute contraindications to dental extraction, especially when presented with an acutely symptomatic tooth. However, there are circumstances where a patient should be better optimized before extraction is performed. In all cases, a thorough risk vs. benefit discussion of tooth removal must be had with the patient. This discussion must encompass an understanding of the patient's medical background and the specific tooth or teeth being extracted.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 675
retrieved_docs.content: Other OR therapeutic procedures on bone is the classification category that includes the surgical intervention known as total ostectomy of tarsals and metatarsals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: The mentum presentation of the fetus, in which the fetal chin is the leading part during delivery, is classified as a مّجيء وَجهي (face presentation). Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a dental procedure that directly targets teeth (zuby) and is recognized as part of the broader set of dental procedures. Dental procedures themselves are also performed directly on deciduous teeth (zuby mléčné). Disorders of teeth and jaw are categorized under the parent concept of teeth (zuby) and are specifically located in the deciduous dentition (zuby mléčné). Consequently, both the extraction of a single tooth and other dental procedures may be applied to permanent and baby teeth, while disorders of the teeth and jaw primarily affect the baby teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2308
retrieved_docs.content: Normal pregnancy and/or delivery is categorized as several forms of multiple gestation, including twin pregnancy (both delivered and unspecified for a care episode), triplet pregnancy delivered, and quadruplet pregnancy delivered as well as quadruplet pregnancy unspecified for a care episode (noting two similar Arabic expressions for the unspecified category). These specific multiple‑pregnancy classifications are considered instances of “حمل متعدد” (multiple pregnancy), which itself is included in the Standardized MedDRA Query “مضاعفات وعوامل خطر الحمل والمخاض والولادة (باستثناء حالات الإجهاض وولادة جنين ميت) (SMQ)”, a grouping that captures complications and risk factors of pregnancy, labor, and delivery while excluding miscarriage and stillbirth. The SMQ, in turn, is part of the broader category “نواتج متبقية من الحمل” (remaining pregnancy outcomes). Consequently, each listed multiple‑pregnancy type—twin, triplet, and quadruplet pregnancies, whether delivered or unspecified for a care episode—is linked to the same complication‑risk SMQ, situating them within the overall framework of residual pregnancy outcomes and informing clinical assessment and management of normal pregnancy and delivery scenarios that involve multiple fetuses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 2066
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple pregnancy (حمل متعدد), twin pregnancy that resulted in delivery (حمل توأمي، تمت الولادة), triplet pregnancy that resulted in delivery (حمل بثلاثة توائم، تمت الولادة), and quadruplet pregnancy that resulted in delivery (حمل بأربعة توائم، تمت الولادة) are all designated as members of the Standardized MedDRA Query for complications and risk factors of pregnancy, labor, and delivery, specifically excluding cases of miscarriage and stillbirth. This classification highlights that all forms of multiple gestation leading to live birth are recognized as clinical scenarios associated with increased obstetric complications and risk factors, underscoring the importance of careful monitoring and management in maternal‑fetal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2371
retrieved_docs.content: The conditions “حمل بأربعة توائم، تمت الولادة” (pregnancy with quadruplets, delivered), “حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محددة لنوبة الرعاية” (pregnancy with quadruplets, care episode unspecified), and “حمل توأمي، تمت الولادة” (twin pregnancy, delivered) are all classified as members of the broader category “مضاعفات وعوامل خطر الحمل والمخاض والولادة (باستثناء حالات الإجهاض وولادة جنين ميت) (SMQ)”, which encompasses complications and risk factors of pregnancy, labor, and delivery while explicitly excluding miscarriage and stillbirth. This grouping indicates that both twin and higher‑order multiple pregnancies, regardless of delivery status or care‑episode specification, are considered part of the spectrum of pregnancy‑related complications and risk factors, guiding clinical monitoring and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1989
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids for local oral treatment and other agents for local oral treatment in ATC are both classified as stomatological preparations, indicating that each is a type of medication formulated for use within the oral cavity to manage dental or mucosal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1930
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Other OR therapeutic procedures on musculoskeletal system” functions as a classification label for the broader category “other or therapeutic procedures on musculoskeletal system.” Within this classified group, the specific intervention “Reopening of osteotomy site of facial bone” is regarded as a related and possibly synonymous procedure, indicating that it belongs to the same set of musculoskeletal therapeutic actions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 814
retrieved_docs.content: Normal pregnancy and/or delivery is classified as several specific forms of multiple pregnancy, including حمل توأمي، تمت الولادة (twin pregnancy with delivery), حمل توأمي، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية (twin pregnancy unspecified for a care episode), حمل بثلاثة توائم، تمت الولادة (triplet pregnancy with delivery), حمل بأربعة توائم، تمت الولادة (quadruplet pregnancy with delivery), حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية (quadruplet pregnancy unspecified for a care episode), and حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محددة لنوبة الرعاية (quadruplet pregnancy unspecified for a care episode). These specific pregnancy types are all children of the broader category حمل متعدد (multiple pregnancy). Both حمل متعدد and each of its constituent categories are members of مضاعفات وعوامل خطر الحمل والمخاض والولادة (باستثناء حالات الإجهاض وولادة جنين ميت) (SMQ), the standardized query for complications and risk factors of pregnancy, labor, and delivery that excludes miscarriage and stillbirth. This hierarchical classification links normal pregnancy and delivery to various multiple‑gestation scenarios and situates them within a recognized risk‑factor framework, facilitating comprehensive risk assessment and targeted clinical management for patients experiencing twin, triplet, or quadruplet pregnancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and represent a specific form of skin cancer. سرطان الشفة الخبيث, which translates to malignant lip cancer, is a distinct malignant tumor that originates in the lip and is classified as an oral cavity cancer. Although both conditions are cancers, they affect different anatomical sites and are not subtypes of one another; each requires separate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches tailored to its location and biological behavior. Understanding the differences between melanomas of skin and سرطان الشفة الخبيث is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.
